document.title: Taking acute medical imaging to the patient, the domiciliary based X-ray response team

sourceSummary.oneLine: Technical note describing a UK domiciliary X-ray response team using the Fujifilm FDR Xair portable X-ray unit.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Cited literature supporting clinical evaluation or use-context evidence for portable X-ray imaging outside a hospital setting.
keyPoints: The X-ray Response Team used a lightweight portable X-ray unit supplied by Fujifilm, the FDR Xair.
The FDR Xair is described as a compact, highly transportable unit designed for use outside the hospital setting where power may not be available.
The team worked with an RPA and MPE, created new local rules, and expanded HSE registration to cover plain film imaging in the community to adhere to IRR 2017.
The pilot attended 54 calls and reduced avoidable conveyance by 50%, with 27 of 54 patients discharged on scene and kept at home.

evidence.claim: The document is a technical note about a domiciliary based X-ray response team.
evidence.evidenceText: Technical note Taking acute medical imaging to the patient, the domiciliary based X-ray response team
evidence.pageTarget: Taking acute medical imaging to the patient, the domiciliary based X-ray response team

evidence.claim: The article was available online on 2 February 2022.
evidence.pageTarget: Taking acute medical imaging to the patient, the domiciliary based X-ray response team

evidence.claim: The X-ray response team used a Fujifilm FDR Xair portable X-ray unit.
evidence.evidenceText: specialised diagnostic equipment including a lightweight portable X-ray unit supplied by Fujifilm, the FDR Xair

evidence.claim: The FDR Xair is described as a compact transportable unit for use outside hospital settings where power may not be available.
evidence.evidenceText: the FDR Xair is a highly transportable compact unit designed to be used outside of the hospital setting where a power source may not be available that only weighs 3.5 kg

evidence.claim: The FDR Xair maximum output is stated as 90 kV and 2.5 mAs.
evidence.evidenceText: the maximum output is 90 kV (kV) 2.5 mAs

evidence.claim: The Trust expanded HSE registration to cover plain film imaging in the community to adhere to IRR 2017.
evidence.evidenceText: the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) registration for the Trust was expanded to cover plain film imaging in the community to adhere to the Ionising Radiation Regulations (IRR) 2017

evidence.claim: Community locations required a risk assessment performed by a radiographer after RPS training.
evidence.evidenceText: All community locations required a risk assessment that would be performed by the Radiographer after receiving Radiation Protection Supervisor (RPS) training.

evidence.claim: Radiation safety controls included creating a controlled area, directing the primary beam at a solid floor or brick wall, and maintaining a clear 2 m distance around the primary beam and patient.
evidence.evidenceText: This included creating a controlled area, ensuring that the primary beam was directed at a solid floor or brick wall and maintaining a clear 2 m distance around the primary beam and patient.

evidence.claim: The MPE developed a daily QA schedule including a weekly lead shielding test and testing after a triggered shock detector.
evidence.evidenceText: The MPE developed a daily QA schedule for the equipment which included a weekly lead shielding test that would also to be carried out in the event of a triggered shock detector.

evidence.claim: During the pilot, 56 patients were referred, 54 were attended, and 27 admissions were avoided.
The authors concluded that proof of concept was achieved and conveyance was reduced by 50%.
evidence.evidenceText: Proof on concept was achieved as the team reduced conveyance by 50%.
